2005.assessment:
Malawi maintained full central government control over its territory throughout 2005.
The country has no active insurgencies or armed non-state actors challenging state authority.
President Bingu wa Mutharika governed over a stable, compact state with effective
administrative reach across all three regions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006, Malawi

2006.assessment:
Malawi continued to exercise full territorial control in 2006. No armed groups
or insurgencies challenged state authority. The government maintained effective
presence across the country's densely populated regions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007, Malawi

2007.assessment:
Malawi maintained full central government control throughout 2007. No territorial
challenges from armed groups existed. The state administration functioned across
all districts, with the military and police under civilian command.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008, Malawi

2008.assessment:
Malawi maintained full central government territorial control in 2008. No armed
challengers operated within the country. President Mutharika's government exercised
effective administrative authority nationwide.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009, Malawi

2009.assessment:
Malawi maintained full territorial control in 2009. The May 2009 elections
returned Mutharika with a large majority and governance remained stable. No
armed or insurgent groups challenged state authority anywhere in the country.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010, Malawi

2010.assessment:
Malawi maintained full central government territorial control in 2010. Despite
rising political tensions under Mutharika's increasingly authoritarian governance,
no armed or insurgent movements emerged to challenge state authority.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011, Malawi

2011.assessment:
Malawi maintained full territorial control in 2011 despite significant political
tensions and civil society protests in July that were met with lethal force.
The unrest was civil protest, not armed insurgency; the government retained
authority throughout the country.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012, Malawi

2012.assessment:
Following the death of President Mutharika in April 2012 and the constitutional
succession of Joyce Banda, Malawi maintained full territorial control. The
transition was smooth; no armed groups challenged state authority during this
period of political change.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013, Malawi

2013.assessment:
Malawi maintained full territorial control in 2013. The Cashgate corruption
scandal emerged in September 2013 but did not affect territorial integrity.
No armed groups operated within the country.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014, Malawi

2014.assessment:
Malawi maintained full territorial control in 2014. Peter Mutharika won the
May 2014 elections in a contested but ultimately accepted outcome. No armed
actors threatened state territorial authority.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015, Malawi

2015.assessment:
Malawi maintained full territorial control in 2015. The devastating January floods
were a natural disaster requiring emergency response, not a territorial security
challenge. The government retained effective authority across all regions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016, Malawi

2016.assessment:
Malawi maintained full central government territorial control throughout 2016.
No insurgent or armed non-state actors challenged state authority. Peter Mutharika's
DPP government exercised administrative authority nationwide.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017, Malawi

2017.assessment:
Malawi maintained full territorial control throughout 2017. No armed challengers
operated within the country. The civilian government retained effective authority
including over security forces.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2018, Malawi

2018.assessment:
Malawi maintained full central government territorial control in 2018. As the country
approached its 2019 elections, political competition remained peaceful with no armed
actors challenging state authority or controlling any territory.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2019, Malawi

2019.assessment:
Malawi maintained full territorial control in 2019. The disputed May 2019 election
result sparked protests and legal challenges, but these were channeled through
constitutional mechanisms rather than armed conflict. The state retained full
administrative authority throughout.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020, Malawi

Malawi maintained full territorial control throughout the landmark 2020 transition.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021, Malawi

Malawi maintained full territorial control in 2021 under the new Chakwera

effective administrative reach nationwide.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022, Malawi

Malawi maintained full territorial control in 2022. Despite economic challenges

but not territorial control loss.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023, Malawi

Malawi maintained full territorial control in 2023. The country faced ongoing
economic challenges and humanitarian issues including Cyclone Freddy (March 2023),

administrative presence nationwide.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024, Malawi

Malawi maintained full territorial control in 2024. The country prepared for

remained within democratic norms.

Malawi continued to maintain full territorial control as of 2025. General elections

2005.assessment:
Budget transparency in Malawi in 2005 was limited. Budget documents were not
systematically published or accessible to citizens. The Open Budget Survey had
not yet begun (2006 was the first edition). Malawi's budget process was opaque
with limited civil society engagement.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey coverage note: first edition was 2006

2006.assessment:
Malawi scored 47 on the first Open Budget Index in 2006, indicating limited
budget transparency. Some budget documents were publicly available but
comprehensive, timely access was lacking. The IBP rated Malawi as providing
'limited' budget information to the public.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2006, Open Budget Index score for Malawi

2007.assessment:
Budget transparency in 2007 estimated at approximately 47 based on OBS 2006
data (biennial survey). No significant changes to budget publication practices
occurred in 2007 to suggest a change from the 2006 score.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2006 score interpolated for 2007

2008.assessment:
Malawi scored 48 on the OBS 2008, essentially unchanged from 2006. Budget
transparency remained limited. Some executive budget proposal and audit reports
were published but mid-year and in-year reports were not consistently available.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2008, Open Budget Index score for Malawi

2009.assessment:
Budget transparency estimated at approximately 48 in 2009 based on OBS 2008.
No significant changes in budget publication practices occurred. Malawi
maintained limited transparency as in prior years.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2008 score interpolated for 2009

2010.assessment:
Malawi scored 47 on the OBS 2010, a slight decrease from 2008. Budget
transparency remained limited. The score reflected adequate publication
of executive budget proposals but poor mid-year reporting and weak
audit publication practices.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2010, Open Budget Index score for Malawi

2011.assessment:
Budget transparency deteriorated somewhat in 2011 as governance worsened
under Mutharika. The Injunctions Act and broader democratic backsliding
likely reduced commitment to fiscal transparency. Estimate interpolated
between 2010 OBS and next available survey.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2010 score interpolated downward for 2011 based on governance deterioration

2012.assessment:
Malawi scored 42 on the OBS 2012, a decline from 2010. The 2011 political
crisis likely affected budget transparency practices. After Banda's succession,
reforms were underway but the score reflects 2011-2012 practices. Limited
transparency category.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2012, Open Budget Index score for Malawi

2013.assessment:
Budget transparency estimated at approximately 42 in 2013. Cashgate scandal
revealed severe weaknesses in government financial management systems, though
the OBS measures transparency/disclosure rather than integrity. Post-Cashgate
reforms aimed at improving financial management systems.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2012 score maintained for 2013; Cashgate context

2014.assessment:
Budget transparency estimated to be recovering toward 45 in 2014 as post-Cashgate
reforms were implemented. The government adopted the Integrated Financial
Management Information System (IFMIS) improvements and other measures.
Interpolated between 2012 and 2015 surveys.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2012-2015 interpolation; post-Cashgate IFMIS reforms

2015.assessment:
Malawi scored 47 on the OBS 2015, recovering from the 2012 low. Post-Cashgate
financial management reforms improved budget documentation and publication.
The score reflected improved executive budget proposal publication but continued
weaknesses in audit reports and mid-year reviews.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2015, Open Budget Index score for Malawi

2016.assessment:
Budget transparency estimated at approximately 47 in 2016, consistent with
the 2015 OBS score. No major changes in budget publication practices in 2016.
Limited transparency continued.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2015 score maintained for 2016

2017.assessment:
Malawi scored 46 on the OBS 2017, essentially unchanged from 2015. Budget
transparency remained limited. The executive budget proposal was published
but audit reports, mid-year reviews, and year-end reports were inconsistently
available.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2017, Open Budget Index score for Malawi

2018.assessment:
Budget transparency estimated at approximately 46 in 2018 based on OBS 2017.
No significant changes in budget publication practices occurred. Limited
transparency continued as a structural characteristic.

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2017 score maintained for 2018

2019.assessment:
Malawi scored 48 on the OBS 2019, a slight improvement. Budget transparency

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2019, Open Budget Index score for Malawi

Budget transparency estimated at approximately 50 in 2020, reflecting the
positive democratic transition. The Chakwera government signaled commitment

Budget transparency estimated at approximately 52 in 2022. Despite economic

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2021 score maintained for 2022

Malawi scored 53 on the OBS 2023, a slight improvement. Budget transparency

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2023, Open Budget Index score for Malawi

Budget transparency estimated at approximately 53 in 2024, maintaining the

sources.citation: IBP OBS 2023 score maintained for 2024

2005.assessment:
Legal transparency in Malawi in 2005 was limited. Laws were published in
the official Government Gazette but public access was restricted by distribution
and costs. No Freedom of Information Act existed. Court decisions were not
systematically published. The WJP Rule of Law Index did not yet cover Malawi
in 2005.

sources.citation: US State Department, Country Reports on Human Rights Practices: Malawi 2005

2006.assessment:
Legal transparency remained limited in 2006. Legislative procedures allowed
public access to parliament but practical barriers remained high. No systematic
right to information existed. Regulatory frameworks were opaque with limited
public access.

sources.citation: US State Department, Country Reports on Human Rights Practices: Malawi 2006

2007.assessment:
Legal transparency was limited in 2007 with no Freedom of Information
legislation. Laws were published but not easily accessible. Court decisions
were not systematically published online.

sources.citation: US State Department, Country Reports on Human Rights Practices: Malawi 2007

2008.assessment:
Legal transparency remained limited in 2008. The Access to Information bill
was under discussion but not yet passed. Regulatory frameworks lacked
transparency. Public engagement with legislative processes was minimal.

sources.citation: US State Department, Country Reports on Human Rights Practices: Malawi 2008

2009.assessment:
Legal transparency was limited in 2009. No ATI law in force. Government
regulations and policies were not systematically published online. Court
decisions limited in public accessibility.

sources.citation: US State Department, Country Reports on Human Rights Practices: Malawi 2009

2010.assessment:
Legal transparency deteriorated in 2010 as Mutharika's governance became
more opaque. Regulatory processes showed less transparency. The emerging
authoritarianism reduced access to government information.

sources.citation: US State Department, Country Reports on Human Rights Practices: Malawi 2010

2011.assessment:
Legal transparency was significantly impaired in 2011. The Injunctions Act
restricted courts from publishing orders against the government. Policy
processes became less transparent. No ATI law existed. The governance crisis
substantially reduced legal/regulatory transparency.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012, Malawi

2012.assessment:
Legal transparency improved in 2012 after the Injunctions Act was repealed.
Banda's government was more open about regulations and policies. Court orders
could again be published. The overall legal transparency improved from the
2011 nadir.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013, Malawi

2013.assessment:
WJP first covered Malawi in 2013 with a Factor 3 (Open Government) score
of approximately 0.40 (40/100). Laws were partially accessible but systematic
publication and right to information were lacking. Regulatory processes had
limited public participation.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2013, Factor 3: Open Government, Malawi

2014.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 score remained approximately 40/100 in 2014. Legal and regulatory
transparency was limited. The Access to Information bill remained stalled.
Regulatory frameworks were opaque with significant barriers to accessing
government information.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2014, Factor 3: Open Government, Malawi

2015.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 improved slightly to 42/100 in 2015. Some improvements in
government transparency were noted. The new Mutharika government implemented
some regulatory improvements. Limited transparency remained the dominant
characterization.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2015, Factor 3: Open Government, Malawi

2016.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 was approximately 43/100 in 2016. Modest improvements in
regulatory transparency were noted. The government published some policy
documents but systematic right to information was still lacking.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2016, Factor 3: Open Government, Malawi

2017.assessment:
WJP Factor 3 improved to approximately 44/100 in 2017-18. Gradual improvements
in legal transparency continued. Legislative processes had some improved public
accessibility. The Access to Information Act was still not passed despite years
of discussion.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2017-18, Factor 3: Open Government, Malawi

2018.assessment:
Legal transparency remained limited at approximately 44/100 in 2018. The
Access to Information Act remained unpassed. Regulatory transparency was
partial with significant gaps. Government information was not systematically
accessible to citizens.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2019, Factor 3: Open Government, Malawi

2019.assessment:
Legal transparency was approximately 44/100 in 2019. The Constitutional Court
proceedings on the disputed election were notable for their transparency and
public access. Court judgments were published and widely disseminated. This
was a high point for judicial transparency even as regulatory transparency
remained limited.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2020, Factor 3: Open Government, Malawi

2020.assessment:
Legal transparency improved to approximately 46/100 following the democratic
transition. Chakwera's government committed to passing the Access to Information
Act. The Constitutional Court proceedings demonstrated high judicial transparency.
WJP noted improvements in open government practices.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2021, Factor 3: Open Government, Malawi

2021.assessment:
Legal transparency improved to approximately 47/100 in 2021. The Chakwera
government worked toward passing the Access to Information Act. More government
documents and regulations were published online. WJP improvements reflected
democratic transition reforms.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2021, Factor 3: Open Government, Malawi

2022.assessment:
Legal transparency remained at approximately 47/100 in 2022. The Access to
Information Act was technically in force but implementation was partial.
Government regulatory information had improved accessibility. The economic
crisis created some opacity around emergency measures.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2022, Factor 3: Open Government, Malawi

2023.assessment:
Legal transparency maintained at approximately 47/100 in 2023. The ATI Act
implementation continued with partial effectiveness. Court decisions were
more accessible online. Cyclone Freddy response included some transparency
in aid distribution monitoring.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2023, Factor 3: Open Government, Malawi

2024.assessment:
Legal transparency estimated at approximately 47-48 in 2024 based on WJP
2023 data. Pre-election period saw some published regulations and constitutional
framework around elections. Access to Information Act implementation continued
at partial levels.

sources.citation: WJP Rule of Law Index 2023 trajectory maintained for 2024

2005.assessment:
Press freedom in Malawi in 2005 was moderate. Multiple private newspapers and
radio stations operated. State broadcasting (MBC) dominated but private media
was active. Journalists faced occasional harassment but a functioning press
environment existed. RSF coverage for Malawi in 2005 was limited.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006, Malawi

2006.assessment:
Press freedom remained moderate in 2006. Multiple independent newspapers,
private radio stations, and a nascent television sector operated. Some
government pressure on journalists existed but not systematic suppression.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2006, Malawi

2007.assessment:
Press freedom was moderate in 2007. Private media continued operating with
notable independence. Some harassment of journalists occurred but the overall
media environment allowed substantive political reporting.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2007, Malawi

2008.assessment:
Press freedom remained moderate in 2008 with active independent media.
Political coverage was substantive and critical reporting allowed. Some
incidents of journalist harassment by government supporters occurred.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2008, Malawi

2009.assessment:
Press freedom was moderate in 2009. Multiple independent media outlets covered
the election campaign critically. State broadcaster MBC showed bias toward
the incumbent but private media provided balance. RSF ranked Malawi in the
middle tier for Africa.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2009, Malawi

2010.assessment:
Press freedom began declining in 2010 as Mutharika's second term became more
restrictive. Media faced increasing pressure. The government withdrew advertising
from critical outlets and journalists faced more harassment. A Media Institute
of Southern Africa report noted deterioration.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2010, Malawi
Media Institute of Southern Africa, Malawi media freedom report, 2010

2011.assessment:
Press freedom deteriorated significantly in 2011. The government passed
legislation restricting media, withdrew advertising from critical outlets,
and harassed journalists. Several radio stations were fined or threatened.
RSF ranked Malawi poorly reflecting the sharp deterioration. The July
protest crackdown was minimally reported by state media.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2011, score for Malawi
Human Rights Watch, World Report 2012, Malawi

2012.assessment:
Press freedom improved markedly in 2012 after Joyce Banda's succession.
The Injunctions Act was repealed and restrictions on media lifted. Multiple
independent outlets resumed more critical reporting. International media
organizations praised the rapid improvement. RSF score improved significantly.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2012, score for Malawi

2013.assessment:
Press freedom continued improving in 2013. Malawi maintained an active
independent media environment. Multiple private newspapers, radio stations,
and television channels operated critically. The Cashgate scandal was
widely reported by independent media.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2013, score for Malawi

2014.assessment:
Press freedom remained at moderate levels in 2014. Election coverage was
broadly free with independent media reporting on all parties. Some incidents
of journalist harassment occurred. RSF rated Malawi as 'problematic' in the
middle of African rankings.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2014, score for Malawi

2015.assessment:
Press freedom was moderate in 2015 under Mutharika's new government. Independent
media reported critically on the government's flood response and economic
management. Some harassment incidents but overall a functioning press environment.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2015, score for Malawi

2016.assessment:
Press freedom remained moderate in 2016. Independent media continued operating
and reporting critically. Some government pressure on critical journalists.
Media access for drought/food security reporting was generally maintained.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2016, score for Malawi

2017.assessment:
Press freedom remained moderate in 2017 with active independent media.
Critical reporting on governance, corruption, and the VP split from the
ruling party was allowed. The media environment was competitive with multiple
private outlets.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2017, score for Malawi

2018.assessment:
Press freedom was moderate in 2018. Independent media covered political
developments including election preparations critically. Some journalist
harassment occurred but the overall environment allowed substantive reporting.
Malawi ranked in the middle tier globally for press freedom.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2018, score for Malawi

2019.assessment:
Press freedom was moderate in 2019. Independent media extensively covered
the disputed election and subsequent Constitutional Court challenge. The media
played a crucial role in documenting election irregularities and the HRDC
protest movement. Some journalist harassment by ruling party supporters.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2019, score for Malawi

2020.assessment:
Press freedom improved significantly in 2020 following the democratic transition.
RSF noted improvement with the Chakwera government's more respectful approach
to media. The fresh election campaign saw broader media freedom. Multiple outlets
reported freely on the election and transition. RSF score improved notably.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2020, score for Malawi

2021.assessment:
Press freedom continued improving in 2021. RSF ranked Malawi better than in
previous years under Mutharika. Independent media operated freely and reported
critically on government performance. The 'Free' classification by Freedom House
reflected improved press environment.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2021, score for Malawi

2022.assessment:
Press freedom remained at moderate levels in 2022. Independent media reported
critically on the economic crisis, government's handling of the forex shortage,
and corruption allegations. Some harassment incidents but broadly functioning
press environment. RSF new 2022 methodology noted.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2022, score for Malawi

2023.assessment:
Press freedom remained moderate in 2023. Independent media extensively covered
Cyclone Freddy's devastation and the government response critically. Media
freedom organizations noted broadly positive environment with some ongoing
concerns about access and journalist safety.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2023, score for Malawi

2024.assessment:
Press freedom maintained moderate levels in 2024. Independent media covered
election preparations and political competition extensively. Campaign coverage
was broadly free with multiple outlets representing different perspectives.
RSF rating remained in the 'problematic' category.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2024, score for Malawi

2025.assessment:
Press freedom for 2025 not yet fully assessed. RSF 2025 index not yet available
at time of data collection. Based on trajectory and September election environment,
press freedom was expected to remain at 'problematic' levels with broadly
functioning independent media covering the elections.

sources.citation: RSF trajectory assessment based on 2020-2024 data

2005.assessment:
Malawi's statistical capacity was moderate at approximately 56/100 in 2005.
The National Statistical Office (NSO) conducted regular surveys but faced
resource constraints. National accounts and basic demographic data were
produced. However, data quality and timeliness had significant limitations.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Malawi, 2005

2006.assessment:
Statistical capacity improved slightly to 58/100 in 2006. HIPC completion
and improved government funding helped strengthen the NSO. Household surveys
and agricultural census data became more regularly available. Data sharing
with international organizations improved.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Malawi, 2006

2007.assessment:
Statistical capacity reached 60/100 in 2007. The NSO conducted the 2008
Population and Housing Census preparatory work. Data collection systems
were functional. Agricultural production statistics supported key policy
decisions.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Malawi, 2007

2008.assessment:
Statistical capacity was 62/100 in 2008. The Population and Housing Census
was conducted in 2008, a significant data collection exercise. IHS3 (Integrated
Household Survey) data collection was ongoing. Statistical systems showed
moderate capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Malawi, 2008

2009.assessment:
Statistical capacity remained at 62/100 in 2009. Post-census data processing
was ongoing. The NSO maintained regular economic statistics production.
Data quality and timeliness remained moderate.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Malawi, 2009

2010.assessment:
Statistical capacity remained stable at 62/100 in 2010. The NSO produced
regular GDP estimates, price indices, and demographic data. Donor support
from the World Bank and DFID contributed to maintaining statistical capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Malawi, 2010

2011.assessment:
Statistical capacity declined slightly to 60/100 in 2011 as the governance
crisis reduced government resources and focus. Donor budget support suspension
affected statistical office operations. Basic data collection continued but
at reduced quality.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Malawi, 2011

2012.assessment:
Statistical capacity recovered to 62/100 in 2012 as donor engagement resumed
under Banda. The restoration of international support helped fund NSO operations.
Macroeconomic data quality improved as IMF program engagement resumed.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Malawi, 2012

2013.assessment:
Statistical capacity remained at 62/100 in 2013. Ironically, the Cashgate
scandal highlighted the importance of statistical and financial monitoring
systems. Post-Cashgate reforms aimed at improving government accounting and
data systems.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Malawi, 2013

2014.assessment:
Statistical capacity improved to 64/100 in 2014. Post-Cashgate institutional
reforms, IFMIS improvements, and continued donor support helped strengthen
data systems. The 2014 elections required improved administrative data capacity.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Malawi, 2014

2015.assessment:
Statistical capacity improved further to 65/100 in 2015. Malawi conducted
a new Integrated Household Survey (IHS4) in 2016-17. Humanitarian data
collection for the flood response was supported by international partners.
NSO maintained regular economic monitoring.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Malawi, 2015

2016.assessment:
Statistical capacity remained at 65/100 in 2016. The NSO produced regular
statistics including CPI, GDP estimates, and the drought-related food security
monitoring data. IHS4 data collection was underway.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Malawi, 2016

2017.assessment:
Statistical capacity maintained at 65/100 in 2017. IHS4 data publication
provided important poverty and welfare data. Regular economic statistics
production continued. Donor support through DFID and the World Bank sustained
NSO operations.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Malawi, 2017

2018.assessment:
Statistical capacity improved to 67/100 in 2018. Pre-election administrative
data systems were strengthened. The NSO maintained good data production
practices. Economic monitoring data was regularly produced and shared with
international partners.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Malawi, 2018

2019.assessment:
Statistical capacity remained at 67/100 in 2019. Regular economic and demographic
data production continued. Election management data systems supported the
2019 electoral process. Post-election, data on irregularities was documented
and used in the Constitutional Court proceedings.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Malawi, 2019

2020.assessment:
Statistical capacity remained at 67/100 in the final year of WB SCI coverage
(discontinued after 2020). The democratic transition did not disrupt NSO
operations. COVID-19 affected some data collection activities. The Chakwera
government began implementing new statistical development plans.

sources.citation: World Bank Statistical Capacity Indicator (IQ.SCI.OVRL), Malawi, 2020

2021.assessment:
Statistical capacity in 2021 was maintained at broadly 'good' levels based
on qualitative assessment. The WB SCI indicator was discontinued. NSO continued
regular data production. UNSD reported on Malawi's statistical development
plans being implemented.

sources.citation: UNSD National Statistical System assessment, Malawi 2021

2022.assessment:
Statistical transparency remained at moderate-good levels in 2022 despite
the economic crisis. IMF program engagement required quality macroeconomic
data. The NSO continued regular CPI, GDP, and demographic data production.
Crisis conditions added complexity to accurate data collection.

sources.citation: IMF Article IV Consultation, Malawi, 2022, data assessment

2023.assessment:
Statistical transparency was maintained at moderate-good levels in 2023.
NSO published regular economic statistics. Cyclone Freddy data collection
was supported by international organizations. IMF program required quality
macroeconomic reporting.

sources.citation: IMF Article IV Consultation, Malawi, 2023, data assessment

2024.assessment:
Statistical transparency maintained in 2024. Regular economic monitoring
data was produced. Pre-election period required reliable economic and
demographic data production for policy purposes.

sources.citation: IMF Article IV Consultation, Malawi, 2024
